title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 19 - ‘Spirited Away’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins put Spirited Away at No. 19, the only animated film on the list, and use it to talk through Miyazaki: why they take it over any Pixar, and where it sits in the globalization of American moviegoing.

films.title: Spirited Away
films.note: The list's only animated film and, per Sean, the one Miyazaki slot Amanda had reserved for whichever of his the century allowed. Chihiro, a 10-year-old moving to a new town, watches her parents gorge themselves and turn into pigs at an abandoned amusement park, then works to free them across a spirit world of soot sprites, No-Face, a river-spirit dragon, and the witch Yubaba. Released in Japan in 2001, it became the highest-grossing film in Japanese history (unseated around 2020 by a Demon Slayer film); John Lasseter and Michael Eisner backed the dubbed U.S. release and it won Best Animated Feature. Sean frames it as a bridge, the film that helps open the door to Parasite winning Best Picture two decades later, and both hosts read it as hand-drawn animation at its peak: every frame full of background, foreground, and life, dream-logic that never insists on its own rules.

films.title: The Boy and the Heron
films.note: The late-career return that won Miyazaki another Oscar and grew his cult further; Amanda got acquainted with him around it. Sean calls it aggressively him: the disgusting heron teeth you're forced to look at, proof he never softened.

films.title: My Neighbor Totoro
films.note: Sean's all-time Miyazaki pick, but pre-2000, so ineligible. He contrasts its lightheartedness (Catbus) with the more severe Spirited Away.

films.title: Princess Mononoke
films.note: Sean's other favorite and also ineligible on date. Filed with the young-warrior strand of Miyazaki's work.

films.title: Porco Rosso
films.note: The grouchy-old-man Miyazaki (a pig flying a plane over a lagoon), cited as far less weird and more kid-accessible than Spirited Away. Recently entered Amanda's house.

films.title: Howl's Moving Castle
films.note: Amanda showed it to her daughter and thought it landed a little too soon.

films.title: The Wind Rises
films.note: Named as the most adult of Miyazaki's films; Spirited Away is arguably the next most adult short of it.

films.title: Ponyo
films.note: The reference point for what Spirited Away is not: a film young enough for toddlers.

segments.heading: The animation conversation
segments.note: the Pixar and Disney the pick sits against

films.title: Ratatouille
films.note: Amanda's soft spot and the Pixar she'd have argued for, but never at the expense of a Miyazaki film. Sean grants it's also about the act of creation but doesn't find them redundant.

films.title: Toy Story
films.note: Ineligible on date, but both agree it would be on the list otherwise: a genuinely new kind of movie, a wild act of creation the way Spirited Away was a culmination.

films.title: Soul
films.note: Grouped with Turning Red as the later Pixar films reaching for the existential: a child's format carrying something heavier.

films.title: Turning Red
films.note: Director Domee Shi has called Spirited Away one of her favorite films of all time, the through-line from Miyazaki into how a generation of animators works.

films.title: WALL-E
films.note: Cited among the soulful Pixar films, and the kind of slot Sean says was never really open here.

films.title: Up
films.note: Another of the soulful Pixar entries, and one of Sean's end-of-show recommendations.

films.title: The Incredibles
films.note: Named among the Pixar titles that never made the shortlist.

films.title: Coco
films.note: Never on the shortlist or longlist, per Sean.

films.title: Cars
films.note: Where Sean feels the least Miyazaki in Lasseter's own directing, maybe a trace of the generation-gap theme.

films.title: Alice in Wonderland
films.note: The Disney classic Sean calls the most significant companion to Spirited Away, with Lewis Carroll a clear influence: a girl passing through into a world with its own logic.

films.title: Pinocchio
films.note: Cited for its thin line between human and beast, the transmogrification of children.

films.title: Peter Pan
films.note: The flying, the transport to a Neverland space, creatures that communicate in unexpected ways.

films.title: The Little Mermaid
films.note: Part of the late-'80s / early-'90s stretch when Disney took the animation crown back.

films.title: Aladdin
films.note: Named in that same Disney resurgence, and as the serialized model (sequels, live-action remake) that Ghibli avoids.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: The third pillar of Disney's early-'90s return, before Spirited Away kicked the door open for non-Disney animation to go mainstream.

films.title: Chicken Run
films.note: Aardman's stop-motion, grouped with Wallace and Gromit as the other studios that shared the new animation golden age.

picks.title: The Secret World of Arrietty
picks.note: A Studio Ghibli film Miyazaki wrote, with a somewhat similar tone.

picks.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
picks.note: The noisy, everything-at-once frame feels informed by Miyazaki, down to the debt to classical and Japanese art.

picks.title: Watership Down
picks.note: For the mournfulness and gravity: a whole world of creatures with their own rules, rare in animation.

picks.title: The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe
picks.note: A young person passing through a physical doorway into another spiritual world; Amanda rates the books over the films.

picks.title: The Golden Compass
picks.note: Same door-into-another-world logic; again, she'd point to the books first.

films: Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba the Movie: Mugen Train

The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the.title: The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe

Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba the Movie.title: Demon Slayer -Kimetsu no Yaiba- The Movie: Mugen Train
